E-Commerce
A Women’s Fashion E-Commerce Web Application
Team Memebers
Shilpi Kumari (25MCA0038)
Anjana Ann Binoy (25MCA0163)
Aribah Mariyam (25MCA0128)
C.S.Gaayathri (25MCA0193)
Ayushi Kumari (25MCA0177)
a. Software Requirement
1.1 Introduction
The Microservices-Based E-commerce System is a web application designed to
facilitate online buying and selling of products. The system allows users to register,
browse products, place orders, communicate with sellers, and receive notifications.
Unlike traditional monolithic systems, this project follows a microservices
architecture, where each service is independent and handles a specific functionality.
This improves scalability, maintainability, and flexibility.
The system is developed using:
• React / Typescript for frontend
• Node.js with Express.js for backend
• MongoDB for data storage
1.2 Functional Requirements

User Management
• User registration and login
• Role-based access (Buyer, Seller, Admin)
• Authentication and authorization
Product Management
• Sellers can add, update, delete products
• Buyers can browse and search products
Order Management
• Buyers can place orders
• Order tracking functionality
• Order status updates (Pending, Shipped, Delivered)
Inventory Management
• Maintain product stock levels
• Automatic stock updates after orders
Notification System
• Notify sellers when orders are placed
• Notify users about order updates
Chat System
• Real-time messaging between buyer and seller
• Allow buyers to ask product-related questions
• Help sellers respond quickly to customers
1.3 Non-Functional Requirements
• Scalability: Each service can scale independently
• Performance: Faster response due to distributed services
• Security: JWT-based authentication
• Availability: Services run independently on different ports
• Maintainability: Easy to update or modify individual services
• Reliability: Failure of one service does not affect others
1.4 Assumptions and Constraints
Assumptions
• Users have internet access
• Each service runs independently

• MongoDB is available for all services
Constraints
• No API Gateway used
• Services communicate via REST APIs only
• Limited to basic real-time chat (no WebSockets)
b. Layout Design / System Architecture
2.1 Architecture Explanation
• The system follows a Microservices Architecture where each service is
independently developed and deployed. Each service runs on a separate port
and communicates using REST APIs.
• There is no API Gateway, so the frontend directly interacts with individual
services using Axios.
2.2 Technology Stack
• Frontend: React / Next.js
• Backend: Node.js + Express
• Database: MongoDB

• API Communication: REST (Axios)
• Authentication: JWT
Step-by-Step Architectural Flow of the System
The flow starts from the frontend, where the user logs in using the User Service. Then
products are fetched from the Product Service. When the user places an order, the Order
Service processes it, updates inventory, and triggers notifications. Chat Service is used for
communication.
• User Service → Handles authentication and roles
• Product Service → Manages product data
• Order Service → Processes orders
• Inventory Service → Manages stock
• Notification Service → Sends alerts
• Chat Service → Enables communication
Database Design
The system follows a database-per-service approach, where each
microservice manages its own data independently using MongoDB. This
ensures loose coupling, better scalability, and easier maintenance of the
system.

Each microservice maintains its own database collection. Data flows from users to products,
then to orders, which updates inventory and triggers notifications, while chat messages are
stored separately. Additionally, Redis is used as a caching layer to store frequently accessed
data, improving system performance and reducing database load. This ensures modular,
scalable, and efficient data handling.

The ER diagram shows how users, products, orders, inventory, notifications, and messages
are related using primary and foreign keys to manage data efficiently.

D. Execution Screenshots
• Login/Register page

The login and registration module
allows users to create an account
and access the system as a Buyer,
Seller, or Admin, ensuring role-
based authentication and access
control.

• Product Listing (Buyer View)
Buyers can browse and view available products with details such as price, description, and
seller information.

• Product Creation (Seller Panel)
Sellers can add new products by providing product details such as name, price, description,
and stock information.

• Order Placement (Buyer)
Buyers can place orders for selected products, which are processed by the Order Service and
stored in the system.

e. Conclusion
The Microservices-Based E-commerce System successfully demonstrates the implementation
of a scalable and modular web application using the MERN stack. The system is designed
with independent microservices such as User, Product, Order, Inventory, Notification, and
Chat services, each handling a specific functionality.
By using a microservices architecture, the system achieves better scalability, flexibility, and
fault isolation compared to traditional monolithic systems. The application supports essential
e-commerce features including user authentication, product management, order processing,
inventory tracking, notifications, and buyer-seller communication.
Additionally, the use of REST APIs for communication and Redis for caching enhances system
performance and responsiveness. Overall, the project provides a practical understanding of
modern system design and real-world application development.

Future Work
The system can be further enhanced with additional features and improvements to make it
more robust and production-ready:
• Integration of an API Gateway for centralized request handling
• Implementation of real-time chat using WebSockets (Socket.IO)
• Integration of payment gateway for online transactions
• Addition of recommendation system based on user behavior
• Offers during special occasions
